paragraphs.body: No Chip on the Cup

annotations.comment: Interested in this opening and the title is great.

paragraphs.body: We were 17 years old at the annual back-to-school bonfire in Marshall Dump when Nat’s eyes slid over me. I had sat down in a random part of the circle we seniors had made and was passing over a soft lavender chrysanthemum to the girl between us. It was tradition to share the symbol of well-being in hopes of having a successful school year. She’ll tell you differently but I definitely did not make eye contact with her, I wasn’t someone who looked people in the eye when they were examining me.

annotations.comment: You might make it easier on yourself with a simpler verb tense: I sat down or I was sitting down… we seniors made etc.
Because Nat could be a boy’s name, I wasn’t sure who the “she” referred to here was — Nat or the girl between them.

paragraphs.body: I was wearing a white shirt with simple laces across the edge of the sleeves, a present from my mom’s friend whom I saw once every five years, and a plain black skirt, a birthday gift from my dad’s girlfriend who, for whatever reason, really needed me to like her. I didn’t know why she cared so much, it didn’t matter if I liked her or not, my dad would still keep dating her.

annotations.text: from my mom’s friend whom I saw once every five years,
annotations.comment: Maybe you don’t need this detail? It’s a little confusing and breaks the flow of the story.
Now this helps me contextualize the situation a bit more.

paragraphs.body: That night, Nat had blown smoke into my face, a tinge of earthiness and skunkiness combined with the burning wood from 25 feet away from us infiltrating my nostrils, before saying “Hi, I’m Violet.” She took out her hand as though she actually wanted to shake mine.

annotations.comment: Maybe “stuck out”?

paragraphs.body: “Sami.”

She shook out her still-hanging hand, tore a petal off of the chrysanthemum that I hadn’t realized was between her fingers by that point, handed the flower to the next person, and then pressed the petal into my hand. “Here. You look like you need double the luck.”

annotations.comment: Not sure what this means. I’m a little unclear on the action here. Is Nat reaching past the girl to shake hands, or is this after?
I wondered if the narrator has anything to say to that. Feels like a loaded comment!

paragraphs.body: The first day of classes, she was in my poetry class, and the only seat available was next to her. Throughout the next few months, we slowly became friends, where we would message each other about non-class-related things. Then a little closer: she told me about her absent-minded mom and the dad she’s sure couldn’t have procreated her (“None of my genes align with his, Sami!”) and I told her about my mom (“She’s sometimes really mean.” That was one of the only three times Nat had hugged me.). There wasn’t anything to say about my dad so he was never discussed.

annotations.comment: Is the narrator reluctant to get to know Nat?
Maybe “fathered” here?

paragraphs.body: I never called her my best friend because, for one, there were certain aspects of her that bothered me. She had a habit of going on her phone when I was telling a long-winded story and not being responsive when she was mad at me (including when I didn’t realize what I had done to upset her). And, for another, she already had a best friend, Anya, from when she lived in Arkansas.

annotations.comment: I really enjoyed this paragraph. I like the narrator’s clear vision.

paragraphs.body: So I was always careful about how much I told her. I never wanted to give her any more energy than she was giving me. I would only tell her things about myself if she was vulnerable. Anytime I accidentally overdid it on my end, I created further distance by ensuring things remained surface-level for a good bit. And it was a roller coaster: there would be times when I was excited because it seemed as though Nat and I were closer but then in other instances, she would do something, like not continue a discussion on my uncomfortable encounter with someone for longer than what I assumed we would have. I would question whether we were actually close friends.

annotations.text: never wanted to give her any more energy than she was giving me.
annotations.comment: This is such an interesting, and possibly a little sad, statement.
annotations.context_left: w much I told her. I

annotations.text: she would do something, like not continue a discussion on my uncomfortable encounter with someone for longer than what I assumed we would have.
annotations.comment: This is a little too vague to follow.

paragraphs.body: Other times, I would do something. She was once doing her makeup, her lash curler floating near her right eye, and I had told her “Wow you look so different!” I was not a very smart person. Obviously, people objectively looked different when they put on makeup to some degree, but I didn’t understand that that wasn’t something I could just comment on. And these moments made me spiral about whether I was a good person or not. Because why did I say things that hurt people’s feelings? And was it worse that I didn’t even realize it until after I had said it? Did I not know how to think before I spoke?

annotations.comment: You very nicely get at the narrator’s sense of outsiderness here. It seems like a relatively innocuous thing to say — but clearly Nat took it badly.
annotations.context_left: floating near her right eye, and I had told her “Wow you look so different!” I was not a very smart
annotations.context_right: . Obviously, people objectively looked different when they put on makeup to some degree, but I didn’

annotations.comment: I get the sense that Nat asked some of these questions.

paragraphs.body: In March of our senior year of high school, we both were accepted into Corque College, she went for graphic design and I did bio. “We’re stuck with each other,” I had said, and she gave me her soft smile, the one that I took as a sign from the universe that there was hope we would become closer friends. Maybe I would dethrone Anya.

annotations.comment: The run on sentence here ends up slightly hiding the important information you’re giving.

paragraphs.body: Our freshman fall semester was very fun. Nat and I had decided to go with random roommates to maximize our chances of meeting new people, but we always had each other. Eventually, we formed a friend group of six other people. Things were very even (eight people could split quite nicely), everyone in the friend group was pretty nice to hang around, and we were having a blast doing all those college things. Nat spoke less and less about Anya and my stomach turned in anticipation but at the same time, I still struggled with the idea of her being my best friend. Would she actually be someone I’d feel comfortable with at all times? There were still tiny things she would do that I wouldn’t like, such as rushing me to leave a certain place when she knew very well we had a lot of time, and I would wonder whether my best friend would be like that.

annotations.comment: I felt the author scrambling to keep the story going here. I wouldn’t spend too much time filing in blanks — your writing is better when you give specific incidents.
Again, specifics help.
annotations.context_left: y well we had a lot

annotations.comment: I relate to this and also think, wow, hard to be this person’s friend.
annotations.context_left: tomach turned in anticipation but at the same time, I still struggled with the idea of her being my
annotations.context_right: friend. Would she actually be someone I’d feel comfortable with at all times? There were still tiny

paragraphs.body: Mila, one of the girls in the friend group, who we came in because she lived on Nat’s floor, was a little strange and off-putting at times. One of the first conversations I had with her was about our classes.

annotations.comment: Wording error

paragraphs.body: “You’re workload sounds like a lot!” I had said, after she listed all the computer science classes she was taking.
“Yeah.” She then turned around and started talking to one of the other people.
Later on, I was commenting on the shoes she was wearing and she blinked at me, turned to the next closest person to her, and started talking to them.

annotations.comment: These are very good stories to tell about Mila — tell those about Nat, too. That said, I think we need a lead up to Mila. Your narrator doesn’t quite say that Mila became the issue between her and Nat, but the implication isn’t clear yet. Transition us into this part of the story.

paragraphs.body: But to Nat, she was the nicest and funniest person at Corque. “I really love being around her, Sami,” she said near the end of the first semester. And lo and behold, Nat had loved her so much that they started dating in the middle of our freshman spring semester.

annotations.comment: Ah ha and also uh oh

paragraphs.body: That summer was unenjoyable. Mila stayed at Nat’s during the entirety of the summer. Nat’s parents didn’t care, they were happy to see that Mila was such a “wonderful match” for their daughter. And so if I was around Nat, I was also around Mila, and thus, both of their sickening sweetness. When I shared my concerns with my mom, she said “Leave her then. There are other people.” I debated talking to my dad when he was scheduled to come over for dinner, just so I would have something that could add an interesting twist to the conversation. But he didn’t come, just like he had every single previous time he was scheduled to have dinner with his daughter. His girlfriend would probably call me in a few days to apologize on his behalf. It wouldn’t matter how many times I ignored the call, she wouldn’t stop until I picked up. Or worse, she would show up at the house to apologize and then things would get even worse.

annotations.comment: “Shared her concerns” is an interesting phrase — what are the concerns?
The girlfriend is such an interesting recurrent feature in the story.
annotations.context_left: half. It wouldn’t matter how many times I ignored the call, she wouldn’t stop until I picked up. Or
annotations.context_right: , she would show up at the house to apologize and then things would get even worse.

paragraphs.body: I was also having a crisis about bio and being pre-med. Could I actually be trusted to save people’s lives? I didn’t want to try to risk having that answer be no. After weeks of tossing and turning and asking as many people as I could about their opinions on the matter, I decided to switch to stats.
“What!” Nat had yelled into the phone when I gave her the update. “Why?”
My incompetence was too embarrassing of a reason. “It’s just too long of a process, I don’t want to deal.”
There was some background noise and then Mila’s voice, “She’s what now?”

I sighed silently. This is why we can’t be best friends, she doesn’t understand when I want things to be just a private conversation. But still, a little part of me hoped she would press. She knew that being an emergency physician had been something that I used to read about for fun when I was younger. I would spend countless hours on the weekend volunteering in the hospital (doing dumb things like stapling paper together).

I thought maybe she’d ask why now, but she didn’t, she just said “If you think that’s the best decision.” and then moved the topic to Jordan’s (another person in the friend group) party during welcome week. Anyway, I wouldn’t have been able to answer the question. I didn’t know why now, at 19, I was considering changing my entire career pathway, and not maybe 16 when it would have been easier to shift my focus. But it was another tally for why I had to keep her at arm’s length.

annotations.comment: I feel so sad for the narrator — she is so demanding, but never says what her demands are.

paragraphs.body: I didn’t consciously realize it until later, but I hadn’t spent any one-on-one time with Nat. And I missed her. I shook it off. I can’t miss people in fully committed relationships, who don’t have time for me. So at the start of my sophomore year, I focused fully on the work of my classes and tried distancing myself from Nat and the rest of the friend group. I hosted study sessions and became friends with people in my newfound major. As soon as I became comfortable with that rhythm, Nat called and said “It’s been too long since I’ve seen you. Let’s crochet together.” She knew my secret. I loved crocheting to a fault. It helped me breathe.

annotations.comment: Assume that this means “recently” or “since she started dating Mila” or something?
Maybe we could see them crochet together earlier? It’s a sweeter image of them than I had before this.

paragraphs.body: On the day of crocheting, I saw a third person in the room. Mila’s here? I couldn’t say it out loud because that would have been weird. So I sat down and began crocheting, trying to appear as normal as possible. Unfortunately, appearing normal means talking to people I wasn’t expecting or wanting to have to talk to. It was working, though, until all of a sudden, Mila said, “Sami, can I read your poem? Nat said you’re a really good poet.”

Obviously, I couldn’t tell him the truth so I said “Yeah, sure.” Nat handed him the poem I had printed for her to look at and give me feedback on. It was about something random, the speaker was going to the beach with her dad when she was six years old and it was one of the last moments she had gotten with him before he just stopped caring, or something like that who knows what the heck I was thinking when I wrote it. I didn’t look at Mila’s face, I just kept my eyes on my hands and bit my lips to stop myself from reacting.

annotations.comment: I’m so sorry, I didn’t realize until here that Mila is a guy!
The narrator’s voice is really great here — I loved the sense that she’s embarrassed and upset and also a little proud.

paragraphs.body: “It’s kind of…strange? I don’t understand the clam analogy happening and the girl sounds weird.”
I heard Nat slap a rolled-up paper against Mila’s shoulders and her wailing, “Whhhaaattt,” but I couldn’t see anything anymore. The only thing I could think was Shoot, I need to get out of here.

annotations.comment: Wait, is Mila a guy?

paragraphs.body: I wiped my hands against my pants when Mila let out a weird laugh and said, “I don’t know what to do when people cry.”
I didn’t see it actually happen but I knew Mila was giving her she’s-acting-so-weird face to Nat.
“Sorry about that.” I forced a smile. “I have to go, bye.” And I ran away.

annotations.comment: I reformatted this for you a bit to help with the flow of time. It gets run together a bit with the dialogue and action interspersing.

paragraphs.body: Over the next couple of days, I thought about what happened and attempted to be very logical. Technically, Mila hadn’t done anything wrong. And yeah, she was right, I had no right to get so upset over something so insignificant. Because I was scared she’d try to bring Mila along if I invited her for coffee, I cornered Nat after one of her classes (Google Calendar is so handy for seeing people’s schedules) and apologized.

annotations.comment: Hmmm. This is awfully generous towards Mila.

paragraphs.body: She patted my shoulder (a non-hugger), “It’s okay. And don’t worry, I won’t break up with her.”

annotations.comment: What a strange thing to say!

paragraphs.body: I choked. But all I could say was “Okay.”
The three-way hangouts continued and I was always on guard. I couldn’t trust Mila and everytime I tried to get away both of them, Nat would pull me back. Until the end of our sophomore year.

annotations.comment: It is hard to see how Mila is really not trustworthy — can’t quite tell if this is the narrator being wrong or right.

paragraphs.body: “I don’t want to hang out with you and Mila anymore. If we ever hang out, I want it to just be you.” Her eyes landed on my hand and my stomach fluttered, probably because this was the first time I had actually set my foot down. Typically, I just went along with everything and slipped out as sneakily as I could if I ever perceived some kind of problem.

annotations.comment: Give us this scene. When does it happen? What propels the narrator to do this after so long of not saying anything?

annotations.text: had actually set my foot down
annotations.comment: Yeah, I want to know why she finally does.

annotations.text: Typically, I just went along with everything and slipped out as sneakily as I could if I ever perceived some kind of problem.
annotations.comment: We don’t need to be told this.

paragraphs.body: She respected my wishes. Every time we hung out, it was just me and her. Except it wasn’t fully, not the way it was before Mila because Mila was present in every aspect of the conversation. “I went swimming the other day with Mila,” “Mila made me a blanket for my birthday,” “Oh, by the way, Mila told me she doesn’t like your out-of-pocket comments.”

annotations.comment: Yow. Also, I have trouble thinking of the narrator as making out of pocket comments? When has she done that?

paragraphs.body: I blinked at that. “What do you mean?”
“It upsets her, makes her feel like you don’t consider other people’s emotions before you say something.”
By the out-of-pocket comments, she meant the ones I said and then based on the person’s reaction, realized was not a neutral observation. “Oh. Sorry.”

annotations.text: she meant the ones I said and then based on the person’s reaction, realized was not a neutral observation.
annotations.comment: ? Could we see this happen?

paragraphs.body: “I wanted to let you know because I know it’s something you’re still working on.” And yes, I was a terrible person, because I genuinely thought I was funny when I would make those comments. People would sometimes laugh but then other times, they would poke fun and tell me “That’s a crazy thing to say.” And my response would be “Is it?” And they’d laugh even more which would frustrate me further.

annotations.text: say.” And my response would be “Is it?” And they’d laugh even more which would frustrate me further.
annotations.comment: I wonder if this could happen earlier in the story? It feels a little strange to bring up this new issue here.

paragraphs.body: “I need to think before I speak.” She touched my hand, another flutter probably because I was fearful over the fact that I would have to be on guard all of the time. This would be exhausting. Now, I would have to think about what was coming out my mouth all the time, I couldn’t be instinctual anymore. I swallowed. “Thanks, Nat.”

She removed her hand and started talking about Anya. Not my best friend, not my best friend, not my best friend. When I finally went back to my room, I closed the door behind my back, laid flat on my stomach on the ground, and let out a breath.

annotations.comment: Scarlett, I really enjoyed this short story draft. I thought you did such a great job of getting close to this narrator and group of friends — it was complex and mystifying, in some really engaging ways. I wonder if you know for sure who’s correct in this story? By the end, I could definitely agree that the narrator needs to not be friends with Nat, but I wasn’t sure if that was because Nat was right about her, or wrong, if that makes sense. I don’t want to push you towards some big revelation — that Nat has been manipulating her all this time or something cheesy like that — but I do wonder if you have a clear understanding of what has happened, yourself. Beyond thinking through that, I have two suggestions. The first is that you try to tell the story almost entirely in scenes. You have, mostly, but sometimes your narrator summarizes instead of shows us what has happened, and then at other times, the narrator asserts something and then later we see it. For example, that section when she talks about ways Nat can be cruel, she gives a couple of examples, but I think just watching one scene — just a little dialogue in which we see that yes, the narrator is needy, but Nat is also a little cruel might give you more ambiguity and interest rather than just being told things. I also think clarifying and perhaps slightly reordering events might be helpful. We move around in time a little bit confusingly and I don’t think there’s any need to tell the story in any order but chronological, allowing us to see the tension in this friendship form and fester until it (quietly) explodes. And to that end, do you want the narrator to never push back at all? Again, strong work. Happy revising!
